name: Double Stake Pack – For Securing Mature Wire Root Balled Trees
short_description:
Double Stake Pack

Complete pack to securely installing Mature Wire Root Balled Trees. Includes 2 x 4ft stakes, crossbar, PVC strapping, tree cushion, fittings, and QR code linking to our How to Stake & Secure Mature Trees. Recommended for all Mature Trees from Cullen Nurseries.
description:
Double Stake Pack – For Securing Mature Wire Root Balled Trees

Ensure your mature Wire Root Balled Trees are safely and securely supported with our Double Stake Pack – the complete professional solution for tree stability and establishment.

Designed specifically for use with the Mature Range of Trees from Cullen Nurseries, this pack includes everything you need to properly stake and secure your newly planted trees, promoting strong root growth and protection against wind rock.

Each Double Stake Pack includes:

  • 2 x 4ft x 3” Round Stakes – durable and pressure-treated for long-lasting support.

  • Crossbar – connects both stakes for enhanced stability.

  • Heavy Duty PVC Strapping – strong yet flexible for a secure, adjustable hold.

  • Tree Cushion – protects the bark and trunk from abrasion.

  • Masonry Fittings – for reliable installation and fixing.

  • QR Code – links directly to our step-by-step YouTube guide on planting and staking your tree correctly.

Perfect for landscapers, gardeners, and anyone planting mature trees, this Double Stake Pack ensures your trees establish safely and thrive for years to come.

This staking system is required when purchasing trees from our Mature Range — see our Mature Trees Collection for compatible species and sizes.

name: Malus Mokum Crab Apple Tree
short_description:
Malus Mokum 

A compact crab apple tree with rich pink spring blossom and dark red ornamental fruits.

Supplied bare root at 8ft tall, this hardy variety is perfect for Irish gardens.
description:
Malus Mokum (Malus ‘Mokum’)

This type of ornamental crab apple trees is quite striking and admired for its year-round beauty and resilience in Irish gardens. Being  compact & upright it brings a delightful display each season — from its profusion of deep pink blossom in spring to its glossy green summer foliage, followed by clusters of small decorative fruits that provide interest well into autumn and winter.

The spring flowers of the Malus ‘Mokum’ are particularly eye-catching, emerging as vibrant pink-red buds that open to rich pink blooms, making it a favourite for pollinators and a stunning feature in any garden. Unlike some other crab apple varieties, the Mokum produces dark red, almost purple fruits that not only extend its ornamental value but are also attractive to birds and wildlife.

With its manageable size and upright habit, the Malus Mokum is ideal for smaller gardens, ornamental borders, or as a standalone feature tree. It is also valued for its disease resistance and ability to thrive in Irish soils and weather conditions.

At Cullen Nurseries, our Malus Mokum trees are supplied bare root at 8ft in height, available for planting during the dormant season from November through March.

What makes Malus Mokum different from other crab apple trees?
The Malus ‘Mokum’ is prized for its unusually dark red-purple fruits and vibrant deep pink blossoms. Native Ornamental crab apple trees produce bigger fruits and white flowers.

When is the best time to plant Malus Mokum in Ireland?
As a bare root tree, the best planting time is from November through March, while the tree is dormant, to encourage strong root establishment.

How tall does Malus Mokum grow?
The Malus Mokum typically matures to around 4–5 metres with a compact, upright form, making it well-suited to smaller gardens as well as larger landscapes.

Are the fruits of Malus Mokum edible?
While technically edible, the small dark red fruits are quite tart. They are best left for ornamental value or enjoyed by birds and garden wildlife rather than for eating fresh.

images.alt: Malus Mokum Crab Apple Tree Ireland

name: Liquidambar Worplesdon
short_description:
Liquidambar Worplesdon

A hardy, upright tree with exceptional autumn colour,  vivid and lasting foliage.

Supplied bare root at 7–8ft tall, this ornamental tree is ideal for Irish gardens seeking year-round structure and seasonal brilliance.

Available in the bare root season from November through to April.
description:
Liquidambar Worplesdon

Liquidambar styraciflua ‘Worplesdon’ is a superb choice for Irish gardens seeking autumn brilliance and year-round interest. Known for its striking, deeply lobed foliage, the Worplesdon variety distinguishes itself with a more reliable and intense autumn display than the standard Liquidambar styraciflua. From late summer into autumn, its leaves transform through a spectacular palette of fiery oranges, deep crimsons, and glowing purples, making it one of the most impressive ornamental trees for seasonal colour in Ireland.

Unlike the common Sweet Gum (Liquidambar styraciflua), which can be slower to establish, the ‘Worplesdon’ cultivar is noted for its stronger growth, hardiness, and greater resilience to the Irish climate. It also tends to hold its colour longer into the season, ensuring a longer-lasting autumn show. Its upright, pyramidal form makes it suitable for medium to large gardens, avenues, or as a specimen tree in a lawn setting.

At Cullen Nurseries, our Liquidambar Worplesdon trees are supplied as bare root specimens, 7–8ft in height, ready for planting from November to March. As with all our trees, they are grown and selected for quality to thrive in Irish soil and conditions.

What is the difference between Liquidambar Worplesdon and the standard Liquidambar tree?
The ‘Worplesdon’ cultivar (Liquidambar styraciflua ‘Worplesdon’) produces a more consistent and vibrant autumn colour display compared to the standard Liquidambar styraciflua. It is also better suited to Irish conditions, establishing more quickly and holding its leaf colour longer into the season.

When is the best time to plant Liquidambar Worplesdon in Ireland?
As a bare root tree, the ideal planting season is from November through March, when the tree is dormant. This ensures the best chance for strong root establishment before the growing season.

How tall does Liquidambar Worplesdon grow?
A mature Liquidambar styraciflua ‘Worplesdon’ can reach heights of 12–15 metres, with a graceful, upright shape. This makes it a superb choice for larger gardens, parkland settings, or as a striking avenue tree.

images.alt: Liquidambar Worplesdon tree bare root 7–8ft for sale in Ireland

name: Crataegus lavallei Carrierei
short_description:
Crataegus lavallei Carrierei

(Carriere’s Hawthorn) – 6/8 cm girth, 8 ft tall. Glossy semi-evergreen foliage, spring flowers, and orange-red berries that persist into winter.

Hardy, wildlife-friendly, and ideal for Irish gardens.
description:
Crataegus lavallei Carrierei – Carriere’s Hawthorn

Description & Key Features

Crataegus lavallei Carrierei, or Carriere’s Hawthorn, is a highly ornamental small tree ideal for Irish gardens. Supplied at 6/8 cm girth and approx. 8 ft tall, it is valued for its semi-evergreen glossy foliage, which holds well into winter.

From May to June, clusters of white flowers provide nectar for pollinators. These are followed by long-lasting orange-red berries that persist into the colder months, adding colour and supporting local birdlife.

Key features:

  • Semi-evergreen with glossy dark green foliage

  • Abundant spring flowers for pollinators

  • Striking berries that last into winter

  • Tough and adaptable to Irish soils, including heavier ground

  • Tolerant of exposure, wind, and urban pollution

Planting

  • Size supplied: 6/8 cm girth, approx. 8 ft in height

  • Planting season: Best planted from late autumn to early spring while dormant

  • Position: Full sun to partial shade

  • Soil: Adaptable to most soils; thrives in well-drained or heavier Irish soils

  • Spacing: Allow room for growth if used as a specimen tree; for mixed hedgerows, space at 1–1.5 metres

Aftercare & Maintenance

  • Watering: Keep well-watered during the first couple of seasons until established

  • Pruning: Minimal maintenance required — remove dead or crossing branches in winter

  • Feeding: Mulch annually in spring and apply slow-release fertiliser if soil is poor

  • Wildlife benefits: Supports bees and pollinators in spring, and birds with berries in autumn/winter

Why Choose Crataegus lavallei Carrierei?

Carriere’s Hawthorn is a superb all-rounder for Irish gardens. Hardy, low-maintenance, and packed with seasonal interest, it works beautifully as a specimen tree, in urban landscapes, or as part of a mixed hedge or boundary planting. Its glossy leaves, spring blossom, and vibrant berries make it a reliable choice for year-round appeal. Other options for Crataegus trees that compliment Crataegus lavallei include Persimilis and Paul Scarlet Trees.

name: Prunus avium Plena
short_description:
Prunus avium plena

Plena are the double flowering white cherry.

Spectacular spring blossom, no fruit, and vivid autumn colour.

Hardy, low-maintenance, and ideal for Irish gardens.

Available as a bare root trees, 6/8cms, 9ft tall
description:
Prunus avium plena

Plena is commonly known as the double flowering cherry, is a stunning ornamental tree that brings seasonal drama to Irish gardens. In April and May, it produces masses of elegant, double white blossoms that completely cover the branches, creating one of the most impressive spring displays of any cherry tree. Other options for white flowering cherry trees include, Yoshino,  Shirotae or prunus avium which is the Native Cherry.

Unlike fruiting cherries, plena does not produce messy fruit, making it an excellent choice for driveways, lawns, patios, and public spaces. In autumn, its broad green leaves turn brilliant shades of orange and red, adding a second season of interest.

This hardy and adaptable cherry thrives in most Irish soils and is well suited as a specimen tree, an avenue planting, or a feature in larger gardens. Easy to maintain, Prunus avium plena is prized for its combination of beauty, reliability, and practicality.

Prunus avium plena are available as an 6/8cms, 9ft tall bare root trees and are available in the bare root season.

Learn more about Flowering Cherry Trees and the most popular options for Pink and White Flowering Cherry trees.

name: Hornbeam Hedging Plants – Carpinus betulus
short_description:
Hornbeam Hedging Plants

Robust and reliable, Hornbeam hedging is ideal for Irish gardens — thriving in damp soils and known as the best hedging for wetter areas. Provides year-round privacy, structure, and seasonal colour.
description:
Hornbeam Hedging — Best Hedging for Wetter Areas

Description & Key Features

Hornbeam hedging (Carpinus betulus) is a robust, climate-adapted hedge ideal for Irish gardens — especially where the ground can be damp or heavy. It’s considered one of the best hedging for wetter areas because of its tolerance to moist soils and consistent performance in cooler, temperate conditions.

This native (or near-native) species offers:

  • Good height and density within a few years

  • A semi-deciduous habit: leaves emerge fresh green in spring, mature through summer, then turn golden-bronze in autumn (often retained into winter)

  • Strong response to trimming — it easily forms neat, dense walls of foliage

  • Suitability for exposed, shaded, or difficult sites

Hornbeam hedging, like Green Beech Hedging is hardy and low in maintenance, making it a dependable choice whether you’re establishing a boundary hedge, screening, or dividing garden zones.

Planting & Spacing Recommendations

To get your Hedge off to the best start:

  • Planting season: Best planted during dormancy (late autumn through early spring)

  • Spacing: Use about 3 plants per metre in a single row, or for quicker density, a staggered double row

  • Soil prep: Loosen subsoil, clear weeds and grass, and add compost or organic matter if soil is poor or compacted

  • Planting depth: Ensure the root collar sits level with the soil surface

  • Firming in: Gently firm soil around roots to remove air pockets (avoid overcompaction)

Aftercare & Maintenance

  • Watering: Keep newly planted hedges moist (especially during dry spells). Once established, Hornbeam tolerates moderate conditions.

  • Weed control: Keep the base clear of weeds and grass in the first couple of years to reduce competition. A bark mulch layer helps.

  • Pruning: Trim annually (often summer or late summer) to promote fullness and maintain shape — hard cuts are tolerated if needed.

  • Feeding: Light fertiliser applications (e.g. organic pellets) can support vigorous growth in poorer soils.

Why Choose Hornbeam for Wet Soils?

Many hedging plants struggle in waterlogged or heavy soils. Hornbeam is a choice that balances performance and resilience:

  • It tolerates higher moisture better than many alternatives

  • It holds structure and density without frequent thinning

  • Its semi-deciduous nature allows for good screening year-round

If your site is shaded, damp, or has clay or loam soils, Hornbeam hedging often outperforms other hedges.

Alder Trees would also suit a damper soil and location to further enhance your landscape.

name: Coral Berry Hedging Plants – Symphoricarpos orbiculatus
short_description:
Coral Berry – Symphoricarpos orbiculatus

a hardy, low-maintenance shrub ideal for Irish gardens. Produces pink summer flowers and striking coral-red berries that last into winter, with strong wildlife appeal.

Available as potted plants 40-50cms in height.
description:
Coral Berry – Symphoricarpos orbiculatus

Coral berry is a hardy, deciduous shrub that brings reliable colour and texture to Irish gardens. Well-suited to our climate, this tough plant produces small pink flowers in summer, followed by striking clusters of coral-red berries that remain on the branches through autumn and into winter. Reaching 60–150cm in height, Coral Berry is perfect for informal hedges, mixed borders, or naturalised plantings where it will provide seasonal interest and support local wildlife. Birds are especially drawn to its berries, making it a great choice for gardeners who wish to encourage biodiversity. Thriving in both sun and partial shade, and tolerant of a wide range of soils, this low-maintenance shrub is an easy and rewarding addition to any Irish garden. Cotoneaster Coral Beauty is also an excellent flowering plant, however is more commonly used as ground cover for banks etc.

Planting & Care in Ireland

  • Soil: Coral Berry (Symphoricarpos orbiculatus) is very tolerant of Irish soils, including clay and poorer ground, as long as it is well-drained.

  • Position: Will thrive in full sun or partial shade. In shadier spots, it will still fruit well, making it versatile for most garden settings.

  • Planting: Space plants 30-45cm apart if using for a hedge or natural screen. Water in well after planting and mulch to help conserve moisture.

  • Maintenance: Requires very little care. Trim lightly in late winter or early spring to keep shape and encourage strong berrying. Avoid heavy pruning as it flowers and fruits on the current season’s growth.

  • Hardiness: Fully hardy across Ireland and tolerant of exposed conditions. Once established, it is drought-tolerant and resilient to our wet winters.

  • Wildlife: The berries provide food for birds in autumn and winter, while the summer flowers attract pollinators.

name: Sorbus Joseph Rock (Rowan Tree)
short_description:
Sorbus Joseph Rock

Bare Root Trees, approx. 8ft height and 6/8cm girth.

Produces white spring flowers, golden berries, and fiery autumn foliage.

Available November to March/April.
description:
Sorbus Joseph Rock

Joseph Rock is a highly decorative rowan tree, known for its stunning autumn colour and clusters of long-lasting yellow berries. In spring, it produces frothy white flowers that attract pollinators, followed by vibrant foliage through summer. Come autumn, the leaves turn a fiery mix of red, orange, and purple, perfectly offsetting the golden berries that persist into winter, providing food for birds.

Supplied as a bare root tree (8ft in height, 6/8cm girth), available November to March/April, this rowan is an excellent choice for Irish gardens, thriving in a range of soils and offering year-round ornamental value.

add_to_cart.description: Read more about “Sorbus Joseph Rock (Rowan Tree)”

name: Crataegus persimilis ‘Prunifolia’ (Cockspur Thorn)
short_description:
Crataegus persimilis Prunifolia

Cockspur Hawthorn trees approx. 8ft height and 6/8cm girth.

Ornamental hawthorn with white spring blossom, autumn colour, and red berries.
description:
Crataegus persimilis Prunifolia

Commonly called the Cockspur Thorn, Crataegus persimilis prunifolia is a hardy ornamental hawthorn tree valued for its shiny foliage, white blossom, and vivid autumn colour. In May, it produces clusters of fragrant white flowers, loved by pollinators. By late summer, glossy dark green leaves give way to a fiery autumn display of reds, oranges, and purples. Bright red haws (berries) follow, providing food for birds and adding seasonal interest into winter.

This small to medium-sized tree has a dense, rounded crown and is armed with stout thorns, making it both ornamental and practical for hedging or boundary planting. Its tough, resilient nature means it thrives in Irish conditions, tolerating wind, pollution, and a wide range of soils.

Supplied at 8ft in height, with 6/8cm girth, these trees are available all year round from pots, or from November through March/April as a bare root tree. Hardy, low-maintenance, and wildlife-friendly, Crataegus persimilis is an excellent choice for Irish gardens and landscapes. Whitethorn trees are also a fabulous companion to these Cockspur Thorn trees.

name: Prunus Shirotae Mount Fuji Cherry Tree
short_description:
Prunus Shirotae Mount Fiji Cherry Trees

Bare Root Trees, approx. 8ft height and 6/8cm girth. Known as the Mount Fuji Cherry, with pure white fragrant blossom and autumn colour. Available November to March/April.
description:
Prunus Shirotae Mount Fuji Cherry

is a graceful ornamental cherry tree with a broad, spreading form. In spring, it is covered in fragrant, semi-double snow-white blossoms, which contrast beautifully with the fresh green foliage. As autumn arrives, the leaves turn warm shades of orange and red, ensuring year-round garden interest.

This variety is ideal as a statement tree in lawns, gardens, or parks. Supplied as a bare root tree (8ft in height, 6/8cm girth), it is available November to March/April, and is well suited to Irish soils when planted in sunny, sheltered positions.

Learn about more varieties of flowering cherry trees available from Cullen Nurseries.
